perturbation.original_text: Term . Unless otherwise set forth in this Agreement, this Agreement shall terminate on the earlier to occur of: (i) the consummation of a Possi ble Transaction pursuant to a 8 Definitive Agreement, and (ii) two (2) years fr om the date that the Parties terminate their discussions and consideration of a Possible Transaction; provide d, however, that all Confidential Information and Confidential Material s shall continue to be subject to the terms of this Agreement after the termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Term . Unless otherwise set forth in this Agreement, this Agreement shall terminate on the earlier to occur of: (i) the consummation of a Possi ble Transaction pursuant to a Definitive Agreement, and (ii) two (2) years fr om the date that the Parties terminate their discussions and consideration of a Possible Transaction; provide d, however, that all Confidential Information and Confidential Material s shall continue to be subject to the terms of this Agreement after the termination of this Agreement, unless the Receiving Party determines, in good faith, that continued confidentiality is no longer commercially reasonable.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces a subjective element into the termination clause. While the clause initially provided a clear timeframe for the agreement's duration, the added condition allows the Receiving Party to unilaterally decide that continued confidentiality is no longer "commercially reasonable." This subjective determination contradicts the original intent of the clause, which aimed to ensure the ongoing protection of Confidential Information and Confidential Materials after the agreement's termination. The entire clause is about the duration of the agreement. Now, there is a caveat where that duration may be altered, at will.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Term . Unless otherwise set forth in this Agreement, this Agreement shall terminate on the earlier to occur of: (i) the consummation of a Possi ble Transaction pursuant to a 8 Definitive Agreement, and (ii) two (2) years fr om the date that the Parties terminate their discussions and consideration of a Possible Transaction; provide d, however, that all Confidential Information and Confidential Material s shall continue to be subject to the terms of this Agreement after the termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The changed text adds a subjective condition to the termination clause, allowing the Receiving Party to unilaterally decide that continued confidentiality is no longer "commercially reasonable." This contradicts the original intent of the clause, which aimed to ensure the ongoing protection of Confidential Information and Confidential Materials after the agreement's termination. Specifically, the original contradicted text states that Confidential Information and Confidential Materials shall continue to be subject to the terms of the Agreement after termination. The Changed Text allows for an out to that provision, thus directly contradicting the contradicted text.

perturbation.original_text: HIPAA Compliance . The Receiving Party agrees that it will not have access to any patient care information or records of the A ssignors that are subject to the Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act of 1996 (“HI PAA”), absent the execution of a separate agreement that addresses HIPAA requirements. Until the consummation of the Possible Transaction, the Receiving Party agrees that it shall not contact or otherwise communicate with any patients (past, present, or future) of the Assignor s without obtaining the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party and allowing one or more Representatives of the Disclosing Party to be present at the time of any meetings with any such patients. To the extent confidential patient or other information protected by HIPAA is inadve rtently disclosed to the Receiving Party, the Receiving Party shall hold such information in strictest confidence, shall notify the Disclosing Party of its receipt of such information, and shal l promptly thereafter return such information to the Disclosing Party and destroy any and all copies th ereof. This Section 9 shall survive the closing of the Possible Transaction and/or th e termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: HIPAA Compliance . The Receiving Party agrees that it may have access to patient care information or records of the A ssignors that are subject to the Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act of 1996 (“HI PAA”), provided they implement reasonable security measures. Until the consummation of the Possible Transaction, the Receiving Party agrees that it may contact or otherwise communicate with any patients (past, present, or future) of the Assignor s after notifying, but not necessarily receiving consent from, the Disclosing Party. To the extent confidential patient or other information protected by HIPAA is inadve rtently disclosed to the Receiving Party, the Receiving Party shall make a reasonable effort to protect such information. This Section 9 shall survive the closing of the Possible Transaction and/or th e termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: This change directly contradicts the initial restriction on accessing and communicating with patients protected by HIPAA. The original clause strictly prohibited access to patient information without a separate HIPAA agreement and mandated prior written consent for contacting patients. The revised language loosens these restrictions, allowing potential access with "reasonable security measures" and permitting contact with patients after merely notifying the Disclosing Party, thereby undermining the original intention of protecting patient privacy under HIPAA. This creates an in-text contradiction because the clause starts off talking about HIPAA restrictions and access, and ends with creating less restricted actions that the Recipient can take.
perturbation.contradicted_text: HIPAA Compliance . The Receiving Party agrees that it will not have access to any patient care information or records of the A ssignors that are subject to the Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act of 1996 (“HI PAA”), absent the execution of a separate agreement that addresses HIPAA requirements. Until the consummation of the Possible Transaction, the Receiving Party agrees that it shall not contact or otherwise communicate with any patients (past, present, or future) of the Assignor s without obtaining the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party and allowing one or more Representatives of the Disclosing Party to be present at the time of any meetings with any such patients. To the extent confidential patient or other information protected by HIPAA is inadve rtently disclosed to the Receiving Party, the Receiving Party shall hold such information in strictest confidence, shall notify the Disclosing Party of its receipt of such information, and shal l promptly thereafter return such information to the Disclosing Party and destroy any and all copies th ereof. This Section 9 shall survive the closing of the Possible Transaction and/or th e termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The changed text creates a strong in-text contradiction with the contradicted text, which happens to be the same location. The original text strictly prohibits access to patient information without a separate HIPAA agreement and mandates prior written consent for contacting patients. The changed text loosens these restrictions, allowing potential access with 'reasonable security measures' and permitting contact with patients after merely notifying the Disclosing Party. This directly conflicts with the initial restrictions outlined in the contradicted text regarding patient information access and communication, which is a Structural Flaw - In Text Contradiction as the initial agreement is contradicted.

perturbation.original_text: Remedies . The Receiving Party shall indemnify and hold the Disclosing Party harmless from any damages, losses, costs, or lia bilities arising out of or resulting from any unauthorized use or disclosure by the Receiving Pa rty or its Representatives of the Confidential Information or the Confidential Ma terials or other breach of this Agreement by the Receiving Party or its Representatives, including, but not limited t o, reasonable attorneys’ fees, court costs and other litigation expenses incurred in connection with the enforcem ent of this Agreement. Given the nature of the Confidential Information and the Confidential Materials and the damage that would result to the Disclosing Party if informati on contained therein were disclosed to any third party, money damages would not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement by the Receiving Party or its Representatives. Therefore, in addition to all other remedies, the Disclosing Party shall be entitled to specifi c performance and injunctive or ot her equitable relief as a remedy for any such breach or threatened breach, and th e Receiving Party and its Representatives waive any requirement for the securing or posting of any bond in connection with such remedy.
perturbation.changed_text: Remedies . The Receiving Party shall indemnify and hold the Disclosing Party harmless from any damages, losses, costs, or lia bilities arising out of or resulting from any unauthorized use or disclosure by the Receiving Pa rty or its Representatives of the Confidential Information or the Confidential Ma terials or other breach of this Agreement by the Receiving Party or its Representatives, limited to reasonably foreseeable damages and excluding any indirect or consequential losses. Given the nature of the Confidential Information and the Confidential Materials and the damage that would result to the Disclosing Party if informati on contained therein were disclosed to any third party, money damages would be the sole and exclusive remedy for any breach of this Agreement by the Receiving Party or its Representatives. Therefore, equitable relief, such as specific performance and injunctive relief, shall not be available, and th e Receiving Party and its Representatives retain all rights related to the securing or posting of any bond in connection with such remedy.
perturbation.explanation: This perturbation fundamentally alters the remedies available to the Disclosing Party in the event of a breach. The original clause explicitly states that money damages are insufficient and that the Disclosing Party is entitled to equitable relief (specific performance, injunctions). The modified version reverses this, stating that money damages are the *sole* remedy and explicitly denying equitable relief. Additionally, it removes the Receiving Party's waiver regarding bonds. These changes create a direct contradiction within the section itself regarding the type of remedies available, weakening the Disclosing Party's ability to enforce the agreement. First the section talk about that an specific thing isn't enough, and continues saying that this is the exclusive and only thing available, that is the main point of the contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Remedies . The Receiving Party shall indemnify and hold the Disclosing Party harmless from any damages, losses, costs, or lia bilities arising out of or resulting from any unauthorized use or disclosure by the Receiving Pa rty or its Representatives of the Confidential Information or the Confidential Ma terials or other breach of this Agreement by the Receiving Party or its Representatives, including, but not limited t o, reasonable attorneys’ fees, court costs and other litigation expenses incurred in connection with the enforcem ent of this Agreement. Given the nature of the Confidential Information and the Confidential Materials and the damage that would result to the Disclosing Party if informati on contained therein were disclosed to any third party, money damages would not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement by the Receiving Party or its Representatives. Therefore, in addition to all other remedies, the Disclosing Party shall be entitled to specifi c performance and injunctive or ot her equitable relief as a remedy for any such breach or threatened breach, and th e Receiving Party and its Representatives waive any requirement for the securing or posting of any bond in connection with such remedy.
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the contradicted text. The original clause states that money damages are insufficient and equitable relief is available. The modified version states money damages are the *sole* remedy and equitable relief is *not* available. This creates a strong, direct, and irreconcilable conflict regarding the type of remedies, making it a structural flaw that causes an in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Defined Terms . (a) “Confidential Information” shall in clude all information pertaining to the Assignors and their respective affilia tes, employees, salespersons, independent contractors, agents, 2 officers, directors, customers, suppliers, ve ndors, manufacturers, di stributors, dealers, predecessors, successors and assigns disclosed by the Disclosing Party (or by a third party at the direction of the Disclosing Party) to the Receiv ing Party, regardless of the form or manner of disclosure (including, but not limited to, info rmation learned by the Receiving Party from the Disclosing Party’s employees, agents, officers, directors, professional advisors or other representatives or thro ugh inspection of the A ssigned Assets or the books and records of the Assignors), and including, but not li mited to: (i) information that re lates to the Assignors’ trade secrets, products, services, produc t specifications, data, inventions , ideas, techni ques, know-how, past, current and planned research and development, current and planned methods and processes, client lists, current and anticipated client, service provider and consultant re quirements, price lists, market studies, business strategy and plans, business opportunities, computer hardware, software and programs (including object codes and source codes), comput er database technologies and systems, and distribution, marketing, financial, merchandising, customer, sales and/or salary information; (ii) all information concerning the Assignors’ business a nd affairs, operations, finances, assets, liabilities, for ecasts, historical fina ncial statements, financial projections and budgets, historical and projected sales, capital spending, budgets, st rategic plans, marketing and advertising plans, publications, agreements, the names and backgr ounds of clients and consultants and other service providers, key personnel, personnel training, and techniques and materials; (iii) third party confidential information in the custody or care of the Disclosing Party to the extent the same is subject to a duty of confidentiality to su ch third party; (iv) all offering documents and materials (including of fering memoranda, supplements theret o and similar documents); (v) all notes, analyses, compilations, statistics, studies, summaries, interpretations, and other material prepared by or for the Receiving Party which cont ain, are based on, or otherw ise reflect, in whole or in part, any of the foregoing; and (vi) the ex istence and terms and conditions of this Agreement and the existence and content of the discussions between the Disclosing Party and the Receiving Party regarding a Possible Transaction. Inform ation disclosed by the Di sclosing Party to the Receiving Party need not be identified as “confiden tial” to be deemed “Con fidential Information” pursuant to this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Defined Terms . (a) “Confidential Information” shall in clude only information pertaining to the Assignors and their respective affilia tes, employees, salespersons, independent contractors, agents, 2 officers, directors, customers, suppliers, ve ndors, manufacturers, di stributors, dealers, predecessors, successors and assigns disclosed by the Disclosing Party (or by a third party at the direction of the Disclosing Party) to the Receiv ing Party, which is explicitly marked as “confidential” at the time of disclosure, regardless of the form or manner of disclosure (including, but not limited to, info rmation learned by the Receiving Party from the Disclosing Party’s employees, agents, officers, directors, professional advisors or other representatives or thro ugh inspection of the A ssigned Assets or the books and records of the Assignors), and including, but not li mited to: (i) information that re lates to the Assignors’ trade secrets, products, services, produc t specifications, data, inventions , ideas, techni ques, know-how, past, current and planned research and development, current and planned methods and processes, client lists, current and anticipated client, service provider and consultant re quirements, price lists, market studies, business strategy and plans, business opportunities, computer hardware, software and programs (including object codes and source codes), comput er database technologies and systems, and distribution, marketing, financial, merchandising, customer, sales and/or salary information; (ii) all information concerning the Assignors’ business a nd affairs, operations, finances, assets, liabilities, for ecasts, historical fina ncial statements, financial projections and budgets, historical and projected sales, capital spending, budgets, st rategic plans, marketing and advertising plans, publications, agreements, the names and backgr ounds of clients and consultants and other service providers, key personnel, personnel training, and techniques and materials; (iii) third party confidential information in the custody or care of the Disclosing Party to the extent the same is subject to a duty of confidentiality to su ch third party; (iv) all offering documents and materials (including of fering memoranda, supplements theret o and similar documents); (v) all notes, analyses, compilations, statistics, studies, summaries, interpretations, and other material prepared by or for the Receiving Party which cont ain, are based on, or otherw ise reflect, in whole or in part, any of the foregoing; and (vi) the ex istence and terms and conditions of this Agreement and the existence and content of the discussions between the Disclosing Party and the Receiving Party regarding a Possible Transaction. Inform ation disclosed by the Di sclosing Party to the Receiving Party need not be identified as “confiden tial” to be deemed “Con fidential Information” pursuant to this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: This alteration to the definition of 'Confidential Information' creates a clear internal inconsistency. The original text states that Confidential Information includes *all* information pertaining to the Assignors, *regardless* of whether it's explicitly marked as confidential. However, the change limits the definition to only that information *explicitly* marked confidential. The sentence 'Information disclosed by the Disclosing Party to the Receiving Party need not be identified as “confidential” to be deemed “Confidential Information” pursuant to this Agreement', this is the direct contradiction, as this is clearly stating that there is no need to identify it as confidential, whereas the sentence before clearly states the opposite.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Defined Terms . (a) “Confidential Information” shall in clude all information pertaining to the Assignors and their respective affilia tes, employees, salespersons, independent contractors, agents, 2 officers, directors, customers, suppliers, ve ndors, manufacturers, di stributors, dealers, predecessors, successors and assigns disclosed by the Disclosing Party (or by a third party at the direction of the Disclosing Party) to the Receiv ing Party, regardless of the form or manner of disclosure (including, but not limited to, info rmation learned by the Receiving Party from the Disclosing Party’s employees, agents, officers, directors, professional advisors or other representatives or thro ugh inspection of the A ssigned Assets or the books and records of the Assignors), and including, but not li mited to: (i) information that re lates to the Assignors’ trade secrets, products, services, produc t specifications, data, inventions , ideas, techni ques, know-how, past, current and planned research and development, current and planned methods and processes, client lists, current and anticipated client, service provider and consultant re quirements, price lists, market studies, business strategy and plans, business opportunities, computer hardware, software and programs (including object codes and source codes), comput er database technologies and systems, and distribution, marketing, financial, merchandising, customer, sales and/or salary information; (ii) all information concerning the Assignors’ business a nd affairs, operations, finances, assets, liabilities, for ecasts, historical fina ncial statements, financial projections and budgets, historical and projected sales, capital spending, budgets, st rategic plans, marketing and advertising plans, publications, agreements, the names and backgr ounds of clients and consultants and other service providers, key personnel, personnel training, and techniques and materials; (iii) third party confidential information in the custody or care of the Disclosing Party to the extent the same is subject to a duty of confidentiality to su ch third party; (iv) all offering documents and materials (including of fering memoranda, supplements theret o and similar documents); (v) all notes, analyses, compilations, statistics, studies, summaries, interpretations, and other material prepared by or for the Receiving Party which cont ain, are based on, or otherw ise reflect, in whole or in part, any of the foregoing; and (vi) the ex istence and terms and conditions of this Agreement and the existence and content of the discussions between the Disclosing Party and the Receiving Party regarding a Possible Transaction. Inform ation disclosed by the Di sclosing Party to the Receiving Party need not be identified as “confiden tial” to be deemed “Con fidential Information” pursuant to this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the definition of 'Confidential Information' to include *only* information explicitly marked as 'confidential', which directly contradicts the last sentence of the definition, 'Information disclosed by the Disclosing Party to the Receiving Party need not be identified as “confidential” to be deemed “Confidential Information” pursuant to this Agreement'. This creates a strong in-text contradiction as the clause explicitly states that marking is not needed, whereas, in the changed text it is a requirement.
